speaker: interviewer
text: We're ready to start again. At the end of the last tape, you were talking about the incident where the-- the Children's Center was evacuated. They took children and put them on buses. What happened to those children?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the parents came from work, they-- apparently they-- some news got out that the children were no longer there. So I remember my mom running to-- to-- this-- you know, through the-- through the-- that walk and other women screaming at the top of their lungs, calling the kid's name and calling everybody. And then I found out that these kids were-- were they were gassed. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You couldn't have known at that point where they were taken.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I don't know. I remember even at this early age that why were-- I knew that if you start a bus-- so soon after you start, it goes. But it-- this one, it didn't go. It didn't go. There were I think-- I saw I think about four buses. And it didn't go anyplace. It just-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You were also talking about the-- you know, you're imprecise about dates, and understandably so given your young age. What time of year was it? Do you remember anything about that aspect?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- that had to be-- that had to be early fall, because we were-- we were-- my mother, when we went, you know, she would-- she would put on us those-- a hat and a scarf, you know? And I-- I would be rebelling against her. I say, it's still warm, mama. It's still warm-- warm. She'd say, no, you put this on. It's cold. It's getting cold. It's getting-- so I think that was-- my assumption is that that had to be close to the-- to the early fall.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would that be 1940 or '41?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I can't tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You said that--

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't tell you. '41 maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that your parents could see that that would be a warning.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they take any action at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Very soon, very few days later, we were not-- we didn't go anymore to Children's Center. And my father arranged with-- with from outside people, from nearby village that-- we knew a person that was bringing milk to us every-- very often, I don't know, every Friday I think, from the nearby village. No, he was bringing the milk every day, but every Friday, produce. That's what it was. Every Friday, produce. So-- so he arranged with this person that he's going to smuggle us from ghetto and we will in his-- with his family in Wierszuliszki. That's the name of the village. It's not-- I'll tell you. To me, it sounded like it's so very far. But it couldn't be far. It has to be close to the Vilnius, you know. I'm sure it's still there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he come by horse and cart?

speaker: interviewee
text: A horse-- a horse and a cart and a buggy, full of hay, full of-- of other stuff there, branches. And I-- and my sister and I we were smuggled out of-- of Vilna. I think my parents maybe even paid some to the police. The police was Jewish police by ghetto. It was in-- at night. And then we were put into-- under they hay, under they hay. And he drove off far. And we were driving, driving, driving, had no idea where we-- and that's how he got-- but even before that, even before that, my father tried to explain to me that's the first time, truly, that anybody at that time tried to tell me what is going on, why is it, what's happening.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I remember my father put me on a little stool and lowered himself to the same level. And he told me, Basha, there is a terrible war going on. In order for us to survive, we need to separate. And so you will go with your sister to this-- to this farmer. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] mother is going to stay in ghetto. And there is a special place where she's going to hide.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know that my mom was, I think, expecting then. My mom, yeah, she was expecting then. So she couldn't go with him to the partisans. And my two older sisters and my father were going to go to partisans. I didn't know exactly what partisans even are. But he told me that he's going to another village. And he's going to always look and look upon us and-- and-- and keep an eye on us, you know, and not to-- not to worry. But he told me then to never admit that you speak Jewish. Never tell your name, last name. Just-- just my name is Barbara. My name is Basha. This is my sister. A bomb-- a bomb fell on our house. And-- and I don't know where everybody is and we are lost. That's-- that's about what I was supposed to say, nothing else. Never to say nothing else, never to remember anything else, never to talk about ghetto, never to talk about mother, father, grand-- nobody, nothing. If-- if anybody asked you, just to repeat what I told you. And you always to take care of your sister. I thought I need to literally hold her by the hand. I think that-- that she'll never-- I mean, forever I-- I-- I held her hand. Forever. And so that's-- so that's how-- the separation was awful. The separation was awful. My sister stood-- my mother stood there holding my-- my-- my sister in her arms. I-- I-- I-- I-- I cling to her dress. And I didn't want to go. [CRYING] I just didn't want to go to place.

speaker: interviewer
text: The man who took you, was he kind to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very kind. Very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Kubicki. And we used his name even before. I don't know. My father somehow made arrangements with him that we could use their names. And he had-- he had a large family. There were a lot of kids in there. I think he has five kids. They were very devout Catholics. His wife was a very nervous lady. I-- you know, in the beginning, I was very much afraid of her. But as years went by, now I begin to-- and I sometimes reminisce. And in my mind, well, you know, I-- I can justify this. She was afraid. She was afraid of her own family that if anybody would find out that she's hiding Jewish children, well, there-- there are many examples who were made by the Nazis. When they uncover family that was hiding Jewish children, they were hung in the marketplace and left there for long time so everybody will see them. We made-- they were made example of that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So our life in- in this Wierszuliszki with this family, he was very, very nice. But the lady, like I said, very nervous and upset. And I don't really believe that she really agreed to wholeheartedly to have us there. Can you give any examples of what she said or did that might gave made you a little bit uneasy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she-- from time to time, she would call us brats.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's fairly telling.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So also, my sister was a little bit aggressive with food, you know, when we were sitting in the-- we were sitting in the evening-- well, it wasn't really evening. The main meal was eaten around 1:00. We were sitting there to eat. Instead, my sister to wait until the prayers were finished, she was hungry. So she would grab a piece of bread before that. Well, she would hit her with a spoon. At one point, at one point, even he, who was really very nice man, threw a little knife that he cut, and it slit my sister' s head. You know, she-- because she-- everybody were yelling at her. And everybody was screaming at her. And I told her not to take nothing before the prayers are finished. Well, she didn't-- forgot from one time to another.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was really getting the-- the bad end of the stick. She was getting punished for that. They threw-- even their kids, from time to time, threw something at her, because she grabbed that piece of bread before. If my math is right, she was only about five years old by now anyway.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. She was a little girl. And didn't-- absolutely didn't know-- completely preoccupied when she's going to get food and that's-- and nothing else. And from time to time, she knew-- my father talked to her also not to talk either.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was-- before all this happened, my mother used to grate for her a carrot. And carrot, in Jewish, it's [NON-ENGLISH]. So when she kept crying for that, to give her-- to give her that carrot. From where I would-- from where would I take? You know, I had no such things and wouldn't first of all. And second, I wouldn't know how to do that, how to grate this for her.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so they didn't' really-- they didn't like her very much, plus the fact that she looked like-- like old Israel, was very Jewish child. And so maybe they liked me better a little bit, because I wasn't that aggressive and didn't look like my sister. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: There's another aspect of this. You talked about the fact that the parents had to keep this secret. How about the children? That's five children, asking them not to reveal anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the children, I don't think they knew. I honestly don't think that their children knew that we are Jewish children. I think they were told a-- a story that we are from a different village, that we will be spending summer with them or something like that, some kind of a story. I don't believe. Because I'll tell you, if they would have known, that would be-- that would be the end of us. They were-- from time to time I-- I heard a Jew here and a Jew there, words that the kids even were saying that they heard what's happening with the Jews. And would-- they would come and tell the parents. Well, at one point, every Friday there was a marketplace. That was-- usually, that date was a happy time. Because, you know, kids were looking forward to go to the market. And they would bring their produce to market. And also, they would go there to get some things, too. I overheard the-- the farmer's wife telling him that tomorrow when you go-- and it was early in the morning, usually, when-- when they would go to the marketplace. Tomorrow, when you go, you take the two-- the two brats to-- to Gestapo. I didn't know exactly what's Gestapo, but I knew that it's-- it's affiliated with Nazis. I-- I knew that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I knew it's not-- that this is not good, that this is something it's-- you give them. And for this, she told him that you're going to get the award. You're going to get the award, because there was-- apparently, there was a thing that if you-- if you uncover some Jews or bring them to Gestapo, you will get an award.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the award was, if I am not mistaking-- I know they talked about it, so think that's what it was, some sugar beets, sugar beets. They were talking about sugar beets, so many [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH], they are like so many kilograms. It's like a truckload of-- of the sugar beets.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she was instructing him to take us and when to take us. As soon as he comes, take the-- take them to the Gestapo that apparently wasn't far away from there. Everything-- the whole life was revolving around marketplace. Usually, everything was close by.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- so I felt that this is something bad about to happen. So I started to-- to-- I was afraid to wait till the-- till the morning. So I woke up my sister. Before I woke her up, you know, how human nature, how it is. I knew that the only way I can keep her quiet is to give her something to eat. So I smuggle myself into the pantry. They had a pantry. And they baked their own bread. And the bread was the size-- huge size. The bread was bigger than I am-- I was. And to slice it from a piece like that, it wasn't easy. So I struggled until I sliced that piece of bread. I should have taken the bread and already leave. But then I saw that there was a-- a shelf with what it looked to me like honey, you know, in the jars and a lot of them, several. So I thought, well, I'm-- I'm going to get into this. And I'll get a little bit of honey on this bread and give it to my sister. And only that way I can keep her quiet, and we can just-- just leave. So I did this. And I-- I came. And I woke her up and held the bread right next to her face. And-- and when she saw the bread, she didn't-- she started trying to wake to eat. And lo and behold, few seconds later, a bubble started to come out from her mouth, from her nose. And I thought, oh my gosh, I didn't know that that was soap that they were making, their own soap that looked like honey. Oh my. I thought that-- I thought that-- I wanted-- I tell her not-- I wanted to take away from her the bread and everything. She wouldn't let me. Then she started to make noises. Soap and bubbles and everything she ate. And then I was looking at her, thinking that any moment she's going to die, probably, you know, scared as I was. But still-- still I took her and dress her-- dress her.  We were never undressed really, to sleep. We were-- most of the time, we slept in our clothes. And just we had a little-- it's called in Russian, called [RUSSIAN] we put on. It's like a quilted little jacket. So I put this on her and-- and snuck out of-- of there. The dog barked a few times, but he knew us by then.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we snuck out and went on a road. I didn't know where to go. I had no idea. I don't-- didn't know how far we are from Vilnius. I didn't know where to go, just on the road. And how far we went, also I cannot tell you exactly. It seemed to me like forever, but by then we were very cold. And I noticed that on the road, they had like a place that they're making bricks. So those ovens-- I forgot what you would call them, kill? A kiln?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kiln. Kiln, several of these, and those are very large. And you can go in there, you know, when it wasn't-- they were not active. And it was very warm, very nice in there. Well, of course, we didn't go inside, we just, you know-- and we were by then tired and cold. So we sat down. And soon, I suppose, we fell to sleep. I was awakened by a horse making noises, you know, the horse steps. And I-- when I was awakened, I saw that a priest was sitting and going towards the village. He looked at us, but didn't say anything and didn't stop and continued on this journey. And about probably an hour later, he was coming back. I didn't-- we were so comfortable there, I didn't want to go anyplace. And by then it was already getting light-- lighter. So we still stayed there. And he was coming back. And this time he looked at us, and he stopped. And he walked over.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he looked at us and he asked us-- he looked at my sister, looked at me-- and, are your Jewish? And I say, no. My name is Barbara. This is Leah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And not Leah-- Lucia, that's-- [INAUDIBLE]. This is Lucia. And a bomb fell and exactly word by word what my father told me to say.

speaker: interviewee
text: He smiled. And he had such a kind smile. And he says, would you like to come with me to a safer place? And-- and I looked at him. And I really didn't-- didn't-- didn't think very much about anything. It seems like his smile was so kind. And it seems like we probably will have something to eat pretty soon and maybe a warm place. And we-- I-- I say yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he took us by the hand and put us on the-- in the back of that buggy and drove. And we drove and we drove and we drove and we drove. My sister fell to sleep again, and I did, too. And we found ourselves in a large house surrounded by forest. The house was on outskirts of forest. And then there was a-- a piece of land that was completely barren with-- there was nothing in there, grazing-- grazing fields like and a long driveway to this-- to this house. I remember brick-- brick driveway and several buildings. And when we came, several nuns came out fast. And they fast fetch us, quickly, and-- and-- and took us inside. And we found ourselves in a large room that looked like kitchen. And they washed our faces and hands and fed us. I remember even what I ate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oatmeal. Oatmeal, something that tasted like oatmeal, some kind of a soup. It was warm. And believe me, oh, my, I thought my sister especially will eat the-- the utensils together. She was so hungry. And-- and then we were taken to another room. And they-- they washed us and changed our clothes, different things. Everything was a bit too big, but they tied here and tied there. And-- and we felt very warm, were given a bed. First, we were in the same bed together. But later, they-- they separated us.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to hear more about the convent. But first, just to try to establish in time, what time of year was it when you were found by this priest?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I would say-- oh, it's so difficult for me. I'll tell you the truth. I was always cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So for me, it always seems like it had to be fall or-- or early spring. I don't know. To tell you the truth, I don't know exactly. But I-- since we were through the summer, we were in-- part we were in ghetto. Then part we were in-- in this village.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that had to be either the fall-- it had to be sometimes in the fall. The fall I would remember maybe changes of the leaves and things. I don't remember that. That I don't remember. So-- so-- take-- Perhaps spring.

speaker: interviewee
text: Take your pick.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whatever. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just take your pick.

speaker: interviewer
text: I guess I was trying to get a sense of how long you were with that family, how long they hid you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- probably through that summer. Because I don't remember being winter there, very simply. Winter we were in the convent.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And not even in convent in Ponary, but already in-- in Vilnius in the main convent.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're describing this brick building that you stayed in. You said Ponary was the name of the place.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Ponary, of the-- that was the name of the forest. And there was like a village not far from there. We were not even in the village. We were in like a farm by itself near on the outskirts of the forest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was actually a convent.

speaker: interviewee
text: That looked like a farm, but that was-- that belonged to convent.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which order? Probably we should know that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Benedictines. The order of nun, Benedictines.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the priest or any of the nuns who helped you have names that you remember, so we can remember them, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I remember several names. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: How about the man who found you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The man, that's the priest, that's Father John.

speaker: interviewer
text: Father John, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But there was Sister Teresa that was very-- very young and-- and very nice to us. And she's the one that was spending time with us with that-- on that first place where we were in the convent, the Ponary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] then there was another one. What was her name? I forget her name. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Pauline. Pauline, I think that I [? remember. ?] Paula. Paula. How was life at the convent?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. The-- in main convent, you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, we haven't talked about your moving yet. Right now, I still have you in the small house.

speaker: interviewee
text: In that-- in that farm like.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did they move you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: They move us-- we were told not to venture from the-- from the house by ourselves, not to venture. I was given chores in the kitchen. And I was following the Sister Teresa and, you know, doing things what she told me and not to venture. I don't know. I usually was a very good girl and listened, you know, very conform to what I needed to do. That one particular day that looked such a nice day early in the morning, we were very early up. We were up 6 o'clock, because there was like a mass in the morning. They had like a little chapel. And they went to mass. And they took-- took us there, too. And my sister was not very often there, because she wouldn't sit. But I-- they took me almost every time. Now, after-- after the mass, we would go and have a breakfast. And after the breakfast, it was a quiet time. So during that quiet time, which was still very early, I ventured. I didn't remember that I wasn't supposed to. And I-- you know, I ventured. And the forest was like not-- I mean, right there, very close by. So I went into the forest. Then I went a little bit farther. And you know, very curious, wasn't frightened at all. And I didn't have to go very far from there. I-- I started to hear these noises, these noises, pah, pah, pah, pah, pah, pah, pah, like this. So wherever I heard this, I-- I-- followed that. And it wasn't really that-- that far. I-- I stand behind a-- behind a tree. And I see this group of people undressed.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop the tape here and come back and begin the next tape with this story. Thank you.
